overrides.proposed_override_text: Reviewed product materials identify PTFE nonstick coating in the food-contact layer. This product scores lower under the PAC Safety Score methodology because the score reflects food-contact coating material, use conditions, and disclosure quality.

Caraway uses ceramic nonstick sol-gel coating as its food-contact surface. Some materials are disclosed but key components — particularly the food-contact coating — are not fully characterized. It is used with oven and stovetop heat, including fat exposure. Because key food-contact chemistry is not fully disclosed, that uncertainty is reflected in the score and transparency badge.

overrides.proposed_override_text: This pan's cooking surface is PTFE nonstick coating, the fluoropolymer used in many traditional nonstick pans. Under the PAC Safety Score methodology, PTFE-coated cookware scores at the bottom of our range because high-heat cooking conditions are associated with greater release potential from these coatings. For cookware without PTFE-style nonstick coatings, see the cast iron and stainless steel options below.

warnings.message: Review primary concern may not match current assembly primary concern.
Review primary concern may not match current assembly primary concern.

overrides.proposed_override_text: Lodge's cooking surface is bare cast iron — no nonstick coating, no PFAS, no plastic-based layer between food and pan. Cast iron is an inert metal that doesn't transfer plastic-associated chemicals into food, even under high-heat cooking, which is why it earns one of the highest PAC Safety Scores in cookware. Seasoned cast iron also develops a natural, durable cooking surface over time.

All-Clad's G5 cooking surface is stainless steel — an inert metal with no nonstick coating, no PFAS, and no plastic-based food-contact layer. Stainless steel doesn't transfer plastic-associated chemicals into food, even under high-heat cooking, which is why this pan earns one of the highest PAC Safety Scores in cookware. The exact steel grade isn't published by the manufacturer, which is reflected in the Documentation Incomplete badge, but it does not affect the low PAC exposure.

Caraway's cooking surface is a ceramic nonstick sol-gel coating. Unlike PTFE/Teflon-style nonstick, ceramic sol-gel coatings are not PFAS-based, which is a point in their favor. However, Caraway's exact coating formulation is proprietary and not fully disclosed, so the specific food-contact chemistry can't be independently verified. Under the PAC Safety Score methodology, that disclosure gap — not a confirmed hazard — is what places this pan in the Caution range. It's used under high-heat cooking conditions, which the score also reflects.

overrides.proposed_override_text: Lodge’s cooking surface is bare cast iron — no nonstick coating, no PFAS, and no plastic-based layer between food and pan. Under the PAC Safety Score methodology, cast iron is treated as an inert metal with very low plastic-associated chemical exposure concern, even under high-heat cooking. That is why it earns one of the highest scores in cookware. Seasoned cast iron also develops a natural, durable cooking surface over time.

All-Clad’s G5 cooking surface is stainless steel — an inert metal with no nonstick coating and no PFAS. Under the PAC Safety Score methodology, stainless steel carries very low plastic-associated chemical exposure concern, even under high-heat cooking. That is why this pan earns one of the highest scores in cookware. The exact steel grade is not fully published, which is reflected in the Documentation Incomplete badge, but it does not materially change the low PAC exposure concern.

HexClad uses a proprietary ceramic nonstick coating in the cooking-surface valleys, with laser-etched stainless steel peaks. The manufacturer publishes Light Labs third-party testing showing Non-Detect results for PFAS, PTFE, PFOA, and PFOS — stronger evidence than an unverified marketing claim — but the full coating formula remains proprietary. That combination of real testing plus remaining formula uncertainty is why it scores Good rather than Excellent.

GreenPan’s cooking surface is a ceramic nonstick sol-gel coating over hard-anodized aluminum. This coating category is treated differently from PTFE/PFAS nonstick under the PAC Safety Score methodology, and GreenPan markets the product as PFAS- and PTFE-free. However, those claims were not supported by retrieved third-party test reports for this specific reviewed product, and the exact formulation remains proprietary. It sits in Caution because of that verification and disclosure gap, not because a specific problem was found with the pan.

overrides.proposed_override_text: This pan's cooking surface is PTFE nonstick — a fluoropolymer in the PFAS family. Unlike bare cast iron or stainless steel, PTFE is a plastic-associated coating applied directly to the food-contact surface. Under the PAC Safety Score methodology, PTFE-coated cookware scores at the bottom of our range because high-heat cooking creates greater concern for this type of coating. It sits in High Risk because the score is driven by the coating material and cooking conditions, not by a defect found in this specific pan.
